origin.question:
In the framework of linearized gravity, the spacetime metric is decomposed as $g_{\mu
u} = \eta_{\mu
u} + h_{\mu
u}$, where $\eta_{\mu
u}$ is the flat Minkowski background metric in Cartesian coordinates. Under an infinitesimal gauge transformation $x^\mu_{	ext{new}} = x^\mu_{	ext{old}} + \xi^\mu$, the metric perturbation transforms as$$h_{\mu
u}^{	ext{new}} = h_{\mu
u}^{	ext{old}} - \partial_\mu \xi_
u - \partial_
u \xi_\mu.$$To simplify the linearized Einstein equations, one often imposes the Lorenz gauge condition on the trace-reversed perturbation $ ar{h}_{\mu
u} = h_{\mu
u} -  rac{1}{2}\eta_{\mu
u}h$ (where $h = \eta^{\alpha eta}h_{\alpha eta}$), which reads $\partial^\mu  ar{h}_{\mu
u} = 0$.Starting from the requirement that the transformed perturbation $ ar{h}_{\mu
u}^{	ext{new}}$ must satisfy this Lorenz gauge condition, invert the transformation law to deduce the inhomogeneous wave equation that the gauge vector $\xi_
u$ must satisfy. Furthermore, explain how the geometric properties of the Minkowski background—specifically the vanishing of the Christoffel symbols and the Riemann tensor in Cartesian coordinates—ensure that this deduced condition for $\xi_
u$ takes the form of a standard wave equation rather than a more complicated differential equation.
origin.solution:
1. Express the transformation of the trace-reversed perturbation:$$ ar{h}_{\mu
u}^{	ext{new}} = h_{\mu
u}^{	ext{new}} -  rac{1}{2}\eta_{\mu
u}h^{	ext{new}}$$$$= (h_{\mu
u}^{	ext{old}} - \partial_\mu \xi_
u - \partial_
u \xi_\mu) -  rac{1}{2}\eta_{\mu
u}(h^{	ext{old}} - 2\partial^\alpha \xi_\alpha)$$$$=  ar{h}_{\mu
u}^{	ext{old}} - \partial_\mu \xi_
u - \partial_
u \xi_\mu + \eta_{\mu
u}\partial^\alpha \xi_\alpha.$$2. Apply the Lorenz gauge condition to the new perturbation:$$\partial^\mu  ar{h}_{\mu
u}^{	ext{new}} = 0.$$3. Substitute the transformation law into the condition:$$\partial^\mu  ar{h}_{\mu
u}^{	ext{old}} - \partial^\mu \partial_\mu \xi_
u - \partial^\mu \partial_
u \xi_\mu + \partial_
u \partial^\alpha \xi_\alpha = 0.$$4. Since partial derivatives commute in flat spacetime, $\partial^\mu \partial_
u \xi_\mu = \partial_
u \partial^\mu \xi_\mu$, which perfectly cancels the last term $\partial_
u \partial^\alpha \xi_\alpha$. The equation thus simplifies to the inhomogeneous wave equation:$$\Box \xi_
u = \partial^\mu  ar{h}_{\mu
u}^{	ext{old}}.$$5. The role of vanishing Christoffel symbols and Riemann tensor: In a general curved background, gauge transformations involve covariant derivatives ($
abla_\mu \xi_
u + 
abla_
u \xi_\mu$), and the Lorenz condition involves $
abla^\mu$. To invert the condition for $\xi_
u$, one would need to commute covariant derivatives, which introduces terms proportional to the Riemann tensor (specifically, $[
abla^\mu, 
abla_
u]\xi_\mu = R^\mu_{\ 
u\alpha\mu}\xi^\alpha = R_{
u\alpha}\xi^\alpha$). Because the background is Minkowski spacetime in Cartesian coordinates, all Christoffel symbols vanish ($\Gamma^\alpha_{\mu
u}=0$), meaning covariant derivatives reduce to partial derivatives ($
abla_\mu = \partial_\mu$), and the Riemann tensor is identically zero. This guarantees the commutativity of derivatives, preventing curvature coupling terms from complicating the equation and reducing it to the standard flat-space wave equation $\Box \xi_
u = \partial^\mu  ar{h}_{\mu
u}^{	ext{old}}$, which is always solvable for well-behaved source terms.
